title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Your Own Behavior
meta_description: Discover how to trust that inner nudge when evaluating your own behavior. Stop justifying mistakes and start learning from them with practical psychic intuition tips.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Your Own Behavior
og_description: Discover how to trust that inner nudge when evaluating your own behavior. Stop justifying mistakes and start learning from them with practical psychic intuition tips.
body:
You Already Know When You're Off - You Just Don't Trust It

Let's be real for a second. You've had that feeling. The one where you do something - say something sharp to a partner, procrastinate on something important, eat the third slice of cake when you knew you were full - and there's this instant, quiet nudge from somewhere inside you. A little "uh-oh" or "that's not quite right." And instead of listening, you talk over it. You justify. You explain it away. You tell yourself a story about why it made sense.

I've been there more times than I care to count. And here's what I've learned: that nudge? That's your intuition trying to save you from yourself. Not in a punishing way. Not like a stern parent wagging a finger. More like a friend who knows you better than you know yourself, gently tapping your shoulder and saying, "Hey. Look closer."

The problem isn't that you can't hear your intuition. The problem is that you've been trained to dismiss it when it comes to your own behavior. You'll trust a gut feeling about a person, a place, or a decision to take a new job. But when it comes to looking at yourself - your habits, your reactions, your patterns - suddenly intuition feels unreliable. Too subjective. Too emotional. You want cold, hard facts. But here's the thing: your behavior is not a math problem. It's a living, breathing thing. And it needs a different kind of knowing.

Right?! You've been trying to think your way out of patterns that your thinking mind created in the first place. That's like trying to lift yourself off the ground by pulling on your own shoelaces. It doesn't work. You need a different tool. You need your intuition.

"Your intuition isn't there to give you the answer. It's there to show you where to look. Evaluating your own behavior with intuition isn't about being right. It's about being honest enough to see what's actually there - without the story you've been telling yourself."

Why Your Brain Is the Worst Judge of Your Own Behavior

Your brain has a full-time job, and that job is not accuracy. That job is protecting your identity. Every time you evaluate your own behavior, your brain kicks into defense mode. It wants to keep you looking good - to yourself and to others. So it will spin, rationalize, minimize, and deflect faster than you can blink.

You know those moments where you snap at someone and then immediately think, "Well, they started it"? That's not a character flaw. That's your brain doing its job. It's trying to keep your self-image intact. But here's the cost: you lose the truth. You lose the chance to actually grow. And you keep repeating the same patterns because you never fully see them.

Intuition bypasses that defense. It doesn't care about your self-image. It doesn't care about looking good. It cares about alignment. It cares about truth. When you learn to use intuition to evaluate your behavior, you're not trying to prove you're right or wrong. You're trying to see what's actually happening - without the smoke and mirrors your brain puts up.

Does that land? Because this is the core of it. You can't think your way into self-awareness. You have to feel your way in.

The First Step: Stop Trying to Figure It Out

I know this sounds backwards. You came here to learn how to evaluate your behavior, and I'm telling you to stop trying. But hear me out. The moment you start "figuring out" your behavior, you activate your analytical mind. And your analytical mind is the worst tool for this job. It's too slow, too attached to being right, and too full of old programming.

Instead, start with a simple question: "What do I sense about this behavior?" Not "What do I think?" Not "What's the logical explanation?" Just sense. Feel the shape of it in your body.

Here's a practice. Next time you catch yourself in a behavior you're not sure about - maybe you said something passive-aggressive, or you avoided a hard conversation, or you overreacted to something small - pause. Don't analyze. Don't justify. Just put your hand on your chest or your belly. Take a breath. And ask: "What does this behavior feel like in my body?"

You might feel tightness in your throat. A knot in your stomach. A buzzing in your chest. That's your intuition speaking. It's not giving you a verdict. It's giving you a signal. A signal that something is out of alignment.

This is the foundation. Without this step, you're just guessing. With it, you're actually listening.

How to Separate Intuition From Justification

Here's where it gets tricky. Because sometimes that voice in your head - the one that says "it's fine" or "you had a good reason" - sounds an awful lot like intuition. But it's not. It's justification dressed up in calm clothes.

Intuition has a specific quality. It's neutral. It doesn't need to defend itself. It doesn't get loud or pushy. It's just... there. A quiet knowing. Justification, on the other hand, is busy. It explains. It argues. It brings up evidence from the past. It points fingers at other people.

Try this. Think of a behavior you've been questioning - something you did recently that felt a little off. Now notice: when you think about it, does your mind immediately start building a case for why it was okay? Does it start listing reasons, excuses, or blame? That's justification. That's not intuition.

Now take a breath. Drop the story. Ask yourself: "If I had no reason to defend this behavior, what would I know about it?" Sit with the silence for a moment. See what rises. It might be uncomfortable. It might be a simple "yeah, that wasn't my best moment." No drama. No self-flagellation. Just a clean recognition.

That clean recognition? That's intuition. That's the voice you can trust.

If you want to deepen this skill, there's a book that changed the way I work with this distinction. It's called Letting Go: The Pathway of Surrender by David R. Hawkins. Hawkins breaks down how to differentiate between the noise of the ego and the quiet signal of consciousness. It's a practical guide for exactly this kind of work. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Mirror Test: Using Intuition to See Your Patterns

Once you can separate intuition from justification, you can start using it to see the bigger patterns. Not just one-off behaviors, but the recurring themes in your life. The things you do over and over and wonder why.

Here's a direct exercise. Take a behavior that shows up repeatedly - maybe it's people-pleasing, or procrastination, or getting defensive in arguments. Don't think about it. Instead, close your eyes. Bring the pattern to mind. Let yourself feel the energy of it. Don't judge it. Just observe it like you're watching a cloud pass.

Now ask your intuition: "What is this pattern protecting me from?" Not "Why do I do this?" That's a thinking question. Ask what it's protecting you from. The answer might come as a feeling, a memory, or even a single word. Maybe it's "rejection." Maybe it's "failure." Maybe it's "being seen." Whatever comes, trust it. Don't second-guess.

This is powerful because it shifts you from "there's something wrong with me" to "there's something my system is trying to keep safe." And that's a much kinder, more accurate place to work from. Your behavior isn't the problem. It's the solution your younger self came up with. Intuition helps you see what that solution was trying to solve - so you can finally find a better one.

For a deeper dive into working with patterns and the body's wisdom, I recommend The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. It's not a light read, but it will show you exactly how your nervous system stores these patterns - and how to work with them intuitively rather than just talking about them. See on Amazon (paid link)

What to Do When Intuition Shows You Something Uncomfortable

This is the part nobody talks about. You're going to use your intuition, and sometimes it's going to show you something you don't want to see. Maybe you realize you've been avoiding a hard conversation for months. Maybe you see that you've been using busyness to avoid feeling lonely. Maybe you recognize that you've been playing small because you're scared of what would happen if you actually went for what you want.

When that happens, your first instinct will be to look away. To distract. To judge yourself for having the pattern in the first place. Don't. That's the old habit. The new habit is to stay with the discomfort. To let it be there without trying to fix it or explain it away.

Here's a tool. When intuition shows you something uncomfortable, put your hand on your heart. Take three slow breaths. Say to yourself: "I see this. I don't have to fix it right now. I'm just letting it be seen." That's it. That's the whole practice. The seeing itself is the healing. You don't have to do anything else. The awareness alone starts to shift the pattern.

Know what I mean? You've been so trained to think that awareness requires action. "Now that I see it, I have to do something about it." No. Sometimes the most powerful thing you can do is just witness it. Let your intuition show you, and then let yourself be present with what's shown. The action will come naturally when it's time.

Intuition as a Compass, Not a Judge

The biggest trap in this work is using intuition as a weapon against yourself. You get a hit of intuitive insight - "Oh, I see that I've been avoiding intimacy" - and then you immediately turn it into self-criticism. "I'm so broken. What's wrong with me? Why can't I just be normal?"

That's not intuition anymore. That's the inner critic hijacking the information. Intuition gives you data. The inner critic gives you a sentence. You have to learn the difference.

Intuition says: "You're avoiding intimacy." Full stop. No judgment. Just a fact. Like noticing the sky is blue. The inner critic says: "You're avoiding intimacy, and that means you're defective and unlovable." See the difference? One is a neutral observation. The other is a story with a verdict.

Your job is to receive the intuitive data without adding the story. Just let it be information. "Oh, interesting. I'm avoiding that. I wonder what's underneath that." That's the intuitive stance. Curious, not condemning. Open, not accusing.

This is a skill. It takes practice. But it's the skill that will change everything about how you relate to yourself.

If you want to go deeper into this distinction - between the intuitive self and the judging self - there's a book called Radical Acceptance by Tara Brach. She calls this the "trance of unworthiness" and offers a way out through mindful awareness. It's a beautiful companion for this work. See on Amazon (paid link)

How to Build Trust in Your Intuitive Self-Evaluation

You won't trust this overnight. That's fine. Trust is built through small, consistent experiments. Here are three you can start today.

Experiment 1: The Five-Second Check. Before you react to something - a comment, a situation, a trigger - pause for five seconds. Feel your feet on the floor. Ask your intuition: "What's actually needed here?" Then act. Notice the difference between acting from that pause versus acting from your automatic reaction.

Experiment 2: The Evening Scan. At the end of each day, take two minutes. Don't review your to-do list. Instead, scan your body and ask: "What behavior from today feels most alive in my body right now?" Don't judge it. Just notice. Over time, you'll start to see which behaviors leave a residue and which ones don't. That residue is intuition telling you something.

Experiment 3: The Honest Journal. Keep a small notebook. When you notice a behavior you're unsure about, write down the intuitive hit first - before you analyze it. Just the raw feeling. "Tight chest. Wanted to hide. Felt like I was pretending." Then later, you can look back and see how accurate those raw impressions were. You'll start to trust them more.

These experiments are small. That's the point. You don't need a massive overhaul. You just need to start listening in tiny, consistent ways. The trust builds itself.

When Your Intuition Contradicts What Everyone Else Says

This will happen. You'll use your intuition to evaluate your behavior, and you'll come to a conclusion that doesn't match what your partner thinks, or your therapist, or your well-meaning friend. And you'll doubt yourself. "Maybe I'm wrong. Maybe I'm just being defensive. Maybe I can't see myself clearly."

Here's the thing: your intuition about your own behavior is not the final word. But it is a valid data point. One that deserves to be in the room. You don't have to choose between your intuition and someone else's perspective. You can hold both. "I see that my intuition is telling me X about this behavior. And my partner is telling me Y. Let me sit with both and see what else emerges."

Your intuition is not infallible. But it's also not irrelevant. The key is to treat it as one voice in a conversation - not the dictator, but not the silent child in the corner either. Give it a seat at the table. Let it speak. Then let other voices speak too. The truth usually lives in the space between them.

For navigating these kinds of relational tensions with intuition, I keep coming back to The Gifts of Imperfection by Brené Brown. She writes about how to hold your own truth while staying connected to others - without losing yourself in the process. It's a grounding read for this exact challenge. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Long Game: Letting Intuition Shape Your Character

This isn't a quick fix. Using intuition to evaluate your behavior is a practice, not a destination. You're not trying to become someone who never makes mistakes. You're trying to become someone who can see their mistakes clearly, learn from them without shame, and adjust course. That's a different kind of growth. It's slower. But it's real.

Over time, something shifts. You start to catch yourself earlier. That nudge comes before the behavior, not after. You feel the misalignment rising and you pause before you act. You develop what I call "intuitive integrity" - the ability to sense when you're about to go off track and correct in real time.

That's the gift of this work. Not perfection. But presence. Not being right. But being aligned. You learn to trust that quiet voice inside you more than the loud chorus of shoulds and shouldn'ts. And that changes everything.

So here's my invitation. Start small. Tonight, before you go to sleep, put your hand on your heart. Ask your intuition: "What's one thing about my behavior today that wants to be seen?" Listen. Don't judge. Just receive. That's all it takes to begin.

You already have what you need. You've always had it. You just forgot you could trust it.

If you're ready to go deeper with this work - to build a real practice of intuitive self-evaluation - I've put together a guided assessment that walks you through the process step by step. It's called The Intuitive Self-Evaluation Assessment, and it's designed to help you bypass your brain's defenses and get straight to the truth. Or if you're not sure where to start, take the Quick Intuition Quiz to see which area of your life is calling for your attention right now. Either way, you don't have to do this alone.
